title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a tour guide called Imran talking about a training scheme for young people. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a tour guide called Imran talking about a training scheme for young people. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A tour guide called Imran talks about a training scheme for young guides.
extracts.audio_script: Good morning. I’m Imran and I manage the City History Walking Tours scheme, which gives young people the chance to learn about the town and guide visitors around it. The tours began in response to a school project. Students collected stories about their neighbourhood, and the local council suggested sharing them with tourists. Our office is now inside the renovated [9]clock tower[/9] in the central square, a place that once contained storage rooms but now has a small working area downstairs. From there we organise routes, bookings and volunteer training.||We offer several different walks. Visitors can take a food tour, a riverside route or a tour about local industry. The tour receiving the largest number of requests this season is the one about [10]street art[/10], as new paintings have appeared on several old walls around town. Young guides begin with training rather than immediately leading a group. At their first meeting, they do not study dates or names. They concentrate on [11]public speaking[/11], practising how to speak loudly enough outdoors and how to explain ideas clearly without reading from notes.||Each trainee receives material to help with preparation. We once produced thick information folders, but young people found them difficult to carry during walks. This year, everybody will use a small [12]route map[/12] which includes stopping points and short reminders of important facts. On the day of a tour, trainees must dress appropriately for the weather, but do not need formal clothing. We provide badges; trainees are asked to bring a [13]small umbrella[/13], as tours usually continue in light rain and shelter is limited on some routes.||At first, trainees accompany an experienced guide and help visitors cross busy roads safely. Later, they are each asked to prepare a five-minute talk. Most choose well-known buildings, but one particularly interesting stopping point is the old [14]fire station[/14], which now houses local artists. Visitors often have questions about buildings which have changed purpose over time. In order to make tours available to more people, we have recently recorded an [15]audio version[/15] of our central route. It can be downloaded free by visitors who cannot attend at set times or who prefer to walk independently.||We also arrange special opportunities for trainees. Last summer they interviewed shop owners. This year they will take part in a [16]photography project[/16], recording how streets look at different times of day for an exhibition. After successfully supporting five tours, each young guide receives a [17]skills certificate[/17] describing the communication and organisation work completed. Finally, we are planning an event to bring local residents onto the routes too. Rather than another general open day, July will include a [18]night walk[/18], when buildings on the route will be specially lit and short stories performed by actors. Places are free but must be booked in advance. Please speak to me afterwards if you wish to join the training programme.

dialogue.speaker: imran
dialogue.text: Good morning. I’m Imran and I manage the City History Walking Tours scheme, which gives young people the chance to learn about the town and guide visitors around it. The tours began in response to a school project. Students collected stories about their neighbourhood, and the local council suggested sharing them with tourists. Our office is now inside the renovated clock tower in the central square, a place that once contained storage rooms but now has a small working area downstairs. From there we organise routes, bookings and volunteer training.||We offer several different walks. Visitors can take a food tour, a riverside route or a tour about local industry. The tour receiving the largest number of requests this season is the one about street art, as new paintings have appeared on several old walls around town. Young guides begin with training rather than immediately leading a group. At their first meeting, they do not study dates or names. They concentrate on public speaking, practising how to speak loudly enough outdoors and how to explain ideas clearly without reading from notes.||Each trainee receives material to help with preparation. We once produced thick information folders, but young people found them difficult to carry during walks. This year, everybody will use a small route map which includes stopping points and short reminders of important facts. On the day of a tour, trainees must dress appropriately for the weather, but do not need formal clothing. We provide badges; trainees are asked to bring a small umbrella, as tours usually continue in light rain and shelter is limited on some routes.||At first, trainees accompany an experienced guide and help visitors cross busy roads safely. Later, they are each asked to prepare a five-minute talk. Most choose well-known buildings, but one particularly interesting stopping point is the old fire station, which now houses local artists. Visitors often have questions about buildings which have changed purpose over time. In order to make tours available to more people, we have recently recorded an audio version of our central route. It can be downloaded free by visitors who cannot attend at set times or who prefer to walk independently.||We also arrange special opportunities for trainees. Last summer they interviewed shop owners. This year they will take part in a photography project, recording how streets look at different times of day for an exhibition. After successfully supporting five tours, each young guide receives a skills certificate describing the communication and organisation work completed. Finally, we are planning an event to bring local residents onto the routes too. Rather than another general open day, July will include a night walk, when buildings on the route will be specially lit and short stories performed by actors. Places are free but must be booked in advance. Please speak to me afterwards if you wish to join the training programme.
